original_abstract:
Theories of intraparty competition typically focus on external electoral conditions and formal internal rules. This article moves beyond this "black box" by analyzing the substantive expression of preferences within parties. We argue that while electoral losses drive an increase in intraparty preference heterogeneity, the internal electoral system penalizes candidates who deviate from the party's ideological median. Furthermore, we find that party members grant leaders significantly more leeway to manage competing policy demands when the party is in government compared to when it is in opposition.

To test these propositions, we introduce a new database of party congress speeches from Germany and France, utilizing automated text classification to estimate the relative ideological positions of speakers. The results demonstrate that speeches delivered at party meetings provide empirical insights into the internal preference distributions and power sticks of political actors. Our findings reveal a nuanced relationship between governing status, economic performance, and the degree of intraparty disagreement, highlighting how the institutional context reshapes internal dissent.
humanized_abstract:
While most research on intraparty politics has focused on the external, electoral context and the formal rules governing the contests that parties hold within themselves, a range of questions remain unanswered within the "black box" of party preferences. In this article, we uncover two mechanisms that explain how intraparty politics evolves over time in response to the party's electoral fortune and the formal rules that it has instituted. We show that parties experience an increase in intraparty preference heterogeneity following electoral losses, and that the party's formal electoral procedures impose a penalty on the party's candidates who nominate themselves for nomination contests who drift away from the party's median policy position. In addition, we show that party members are more accommodating of leader's discretion in the management of divergent policy preferences when the party is in office rather than in opposition.

To investigate the theoretical implications of the notion of internal party alignment, we use a new dataset of speeches delivered by German and French politicians at national party congresses and classify the positions of the speakers in the policy space. Our analysis shows that analyzing speeches at party meetings delivers valuable information about the internal preference distributions and power sticks of political actors and delivers a more complex understanding of the relationship between being in government, the state of the economy and party divisions.
